title: The Realization Effect: Risk-Taking after Realized versus Paper Losses
paper_brief: Examines how realizing versus keeping paper losses affects subsequent risk-taking, finding that people take less risk after realized losses but more risk after paper losses.

paper_tests.description: OLS: paper_loss vs realized_loss = 0.3396 (p=0.0000)
paper_tests.result_description: Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.

paper_tests.result_description: Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.

description: Imas (2016) investigates the "realization effect" — how the act of locking in a loss, versus letting it remain on paper, shapes subsequent risk-taking behavior. Participants receive a monetary endowment and play multiple rounds of a lottery investment game, choosing how much to stake in a positively skewed gamble each round. The key manipulation occurs after an early losing round: some participants cash out and realize their loss, while others continue with their losses still on paper — unrealized and unconfirmed. The outcome is the investment level chosen in a later round, which captures how each type of loss experience shifts willingness to take on further risk. The original study finds that participants who realized their losses pulled back — investing less in the subsequent round — while those carrying paper losses actually increased their risk-taking, with the paper-loss group investing significantly more on average than the realized-loss group.

title: What You See Is All There Is
paper_brief: Demonstrates that people neglect the selection process behind the information they observe, leading to systematically biased beliefs even when the selection rule is explicitly described.

description: Enke (2020) investigates correlation neglect — the tendency for people to treat redundant information as if it were independent, thereby over-updating their beliefs. Participants complete a belief-estimation task in which they must infer an unknown state θ, generated as the average of six random draws, by first observing a single signal and forming an initial guess, then receiving additional signals from an information source before making a final estimate. The key manipulation lies in how that information source is constructed: in the Control condition, the source provides genuinely independent signals, while in the Selected condition, the signals are drawn from the same underlying pool the subject already observed — making them correlated and therefore less informative than they appear. The outcome is a "naivete" parameter χ that captures the degree to which a subject ignores correlations among signals, with zero indicating fully Bayesian updating and one indicating complete neglect of redundancy. The original study finds that subjects in the Selected condition display substantial correlation neglect — treating correlated signals as independent and over-updating accordingly — while Control subjects update close to the Bayesian benchmark, a difference that is large in magnitude and highly statistically significant.

title: Cognitive Uncertainty — Risk A
paper_brief: Investigates how cognitive uncertainty -- people's subjective confidence in their own judgments -- systematically distorts economic decisions, producing canonical behavioral biases in both risk preferences and probabilistic beliefs.

description: Enke and Graeber (2023) investigate how cognitive uncertainty shapes the way people value risky prospects — specifically, whether the well-known tendency to overweight small probabilities and underweight large ones might stem from decision-makers being genuinely unsure about their own preferences. Participants state their certainty equivalent (CE) for a series of binary lotteries, each offering a chance to win a monetary prize or nothing — that is, they report the guaranteed dollar amount that would make them indifferent to playing the gamble. The key manipulation is the objective probability of winning: some lotteries carry a very low probability (5%) while others carry a very high probability (95%), allowing the study to examine how probability weighting varies across the probability range. The outcome is the normalized certainty equivalent — the stated CE divided by the lottery prize — which captures how a participant values the gamble relative to its actuarially fair value and thus reveals the direction and magnitude of probability weighting. The original study finds that at low probabilities participants substantially overweight the chance of winning (their normalized CE far exceeds the objective probability), while at high probabilities they underweight it — a classic inverse-S shaped probability weighting pattern that the authors link to the cognitive difficulty of reasoning about probabilistic payoffs.

description: Enke and Graeber (2023) investigate how people update probabilistic beliefs under cognitive uncertainty — specifically, whether stated beliefs systematically deviate from what Bayesian reasoning prescribes. Participants are shown draws of colored balls from one of two bags and must estimate the probability that a particular bag was selected, reporting their belief as a percentage after each draw. The key manipulation is the true Bayesian posterior implied by the evidence: some tasks are designed so the correct answer sits near the extremes (very low or very high probability), while others fall closer to the middle. The outcome is the stated posterior belief, which captures how closely participants' intuitive probability judgments track the mathematically correct answer — and, crucially, whether errors are systematic rather than random. The original study finds that stated beliefs are compressed toward 50% relative to the Bayesian posterior: when the correct answer is a low probability near 15%, participants overestimate it, and when it is a high probability near 85%, they underestimate it — a pattern the authors link to cognitive uncertainty causing people to hedge toward the prior.

title: Motivated Errors — Adding Study
paper_brief: Tests whether observed biases in economic decisions reflect genuine errors or are 'motivated' -- selectively triggered when they serve the decision-maker's self-interest at the expense of charitable giving.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.1667, p=0.0000 | The paper reports a one-sample t-test of the zero_effect variable against 0 within the Self/Charity condition, testing whether adding a zero significantly reduces bundle choice.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero to a charity bundle reduces bundle choice by 6pp in Self/Charity (motivated reasoning condition).

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.0000, p=nan | The paper reports a one-sample t-test of the zero_effect variable against 0 within the charity_charity condition to test whether adding a zero has any effect in the neutral treatment.
paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

description: Exley & Kessler (2024) investigate motivated reasoning — the tendency to misinterpret information when accuracy conflicts with self-interest. Participants make a series of binary choices between an "outside option" and a "bundle," where the bundle is constructed as the sum of several dollar amounts donated to the Make-A-Wish Foundation. The key manipulation is whether a zero is added to the bundle's summands — a mathematically irrelevant change — and whether the outside option benefits the participant personally (the Self/Charity condition, which creates an incentive to undervalue the charity bundle) or benefits charity as well (the Charity/Charity condition, which is motivationally neutral). The outcome is the change in bundle choice rate when a zero is added, which captures whether participants are genuinely confused by the zero or are selectively misinterpreting it to justify a self-serving choice. The original study finds that adding a zero significantly reduces bundle choice in the motivated condition but has essentially no effect in the neutral condition — strong evidence that the errors are driven by motivation rather than arithmetic confusion.

title: Motivated Errors — Correlation Neglect Study
paper_brief: Tests whether observed biases in economic decisions reflect genuine errors or are 'motivated' -- selectively triggered when they serve the decision-maker's self-interest at the expense of charitable giving.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether errors in statistical reasoning are motivated — that is, whether people neglect the correlation in overlapping information more when doing so flatters their self-interest. Participants are shown a target number defined as the average of four underlying estimates, then given one estimate directly along with three "news channels" that each blend that same estimate with one of the others — meaning all three channels share a common component, so treating them as independent leads to systematic error. The key manipulation is whether the direction of correlation neglect happens to benefit the participant: in the overestimate condition, ignoring the correlation inflates one's prediction in a self-serving direction, while in the underestimate condition, the same statistical mistake would deflate it. The outcome is the mean estimation error, which captures how much participants deviate from the correct answer and in which direction — revealing whether self-interest shapes where people apply faulty reasoning. The original study finds a striking asymmetry: participants show significantly more correlation neglect when it leads them to overestimate, and significantly less when it would lead them to underestimate, confirming that motivated reasoning — not just cognitive limitation — drives the bias.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people's desire to reach a particular conclusion — here, to overestimate or underestimate — distorts how they process numerical anchors. Participants answer trivia questions about obscure facts (such as the travel time of light from the Sun to Jupiter, or the weight of the Eiffel Tower), with each question preceded by a randomly shown anchor number of either 20 or 80. The key manipulation is the payment structure: control participants are paid purely for accuracy, while overestimate participants earn more for guessing high and underestimate participants earn more for guessing low — allowing the researchers to isolate whether financial motivation amplifies or suppresses the well-known anchoring bias. The outcome is mean estimation error, which captures whether participants systematically pull their guesses toward the anchor in the direction that benefits them, revealing the role of motivated reasoning in distorting judgment. The original study finds that participants incentivized to overestimate show substantially larger anchoring bias than controls, while those incentivized to underestimate show no meaningful reduction — a striking asymmetry suggesting that motivation amplifies bias in one direction but fails to counteract it in the other.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate motivated errors — the tendency for people to make biased judgments in directions that happen to benefit them — using a classic anchoring paradigm. Participants answer trivia questions about historical statistics, first guessing whether the true answer falls above or below a given anchor value, then providing their own numerical estimate on a slider. The key manipulation is whether the anchor is set in a direction that, if followed, would cause participants to overestimate or underestimate relative to a neutral control — and critically, whether overestimating or underestimating is financially advantageous. The outcome is mean estimation error, which captures not just how far participants' answers deviate from the truth but whether that deviation is systematically shaped by self-interest. The original study finds that anchoring bias is significantly larger in the overestimate condition and significantly smaller in the underestimate condition relative to control, even after design features meant to reduce automatic anchoring — such as requiring a directional judgment first and resetting the slider default to a neutral position — are introduced.

title: Decisions under Risk Are Decisions under Complexity
paper_brief: Shows that classic lottery anomalies (probability weighting, loss aversion) arise equally in deterministic mirror tasks structurally identical to lotteries but containing no risk, suggesting these anomalies reflect complexity-driven mistakes rather than true risk preferences.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-2.5000, p=0.0000 | The paper uses a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (approximated here as one-sample t-test) to test whether CE/SE deviates from expected value (EV=2.5 for G10) against the null of zero deviation, with the reported mean deviation of 7.3 for lottery_G10.
paper_tests.result_description: The fourfold pattern of risk (apparent probability weighting) appears in deterministic mirrors just as it does in lotteries, with the mirror pattern being about 97% as strong as the lottery pattern on average across fourfold tasks.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-1.0000, p=0.0000 | The paper tests whether mirror lambda significantly exceeds the loss-neutral benchmark of 1.0 using a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (approximated here as one-sample t-test) against null hypothesis lambda=1, with the mirror_A10_lambda condition showing mean=1.4 as the key finding that apparent loss aversion appears in deterministic mirrors.
paper_tests.result_description: Apparent loss aversion (lambda > 1) appears in deterministic mirrors even though mirrors cannot produce actual losses, with the mirror lambda being about 80% as strong as the lottery lambda.

paper_tests.description: OLS (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper reports a cross-subject Pearson correlation between each subject's mean absolute deviation from EV in the lottery treatment and their mean absolute deviation from EV in the mirror treatment, which is equivalent to an OLS regression of one on the other (standardized), testing whether lottery errors predict mirror errors across subjects.
paper_tests.result_description: Across subjects, the severity of each anomaly in lotteries is strongly predicted by its severity in deterministic mirrors, suggesting a common behavioral mechanism unrelated to risk preferences.

paper_tests.description: paired t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper uses paired Wilcoxon signed-rank tests comparing lottery vs mirror valuations within subjects across fourfold tasks; the closest parametric equivalent for replication pipeline purposes is a paired t-test on the difference between lottery and mirror CE/SE deviations from EV, with the original effect being the mean difference (lottery_G10 mean 7.3 minus mirror_G10 mean 7.1 = 0.2).
paper_tests.result_description: The fourfold pattern of risk (apparent probability weighting) appears in deterministic mirrors just as it does in lotteries, with the mirror pattern being about 97% as strong as the lottery pattern on average across fourfold tasks.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.9948, p=0.0000 | The paper tests whether mirror lambda significantly exceeds the loss-neutral benchmark of 1.0 using a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (approximated here as one-sample t-test) on the mirror loss aversion parameter against null hypothesis lambda=1, with the reported mean mirror lambda around 1.4-1.55.
paper_tests.result_description: Apparent loss aversion (lambda > 1) appears in deterministic mirrors even though mirrors cannot produce actual losses, with the mirror lambda being about 80% as strong as the lottery lambda.

paper_tests.description: OLS (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper reports a cross-subject Pearson correlation between each subject's mean absolute deviation from EV in the lottery treatment and their mean absolute deviation from EV in the mirror treatment, which is equivalent to an OLS regression of one on the other (same r/p-value), computed by pivoting the data wide per subject and correlating the two treatment-level error measures.
paper_tests.result_description: Across subjects, the severity of each anomaly in lotteries is strongly predicted by its severity in deterministic mirrors, suggesting a common behavioral mechanism unrelated to risk preferences.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=0.7131, p=0.0000 | The paper uses a Wilcoxon signed-rank test of CE/SE deviations from expected value against zero (equivalent to testing whether the valuation differs from EV), which maps to a one-sample test of answer.valuation against the expected value (2.5 for G10) for each condition separately; the closest parametric analog reported here is a one-sample t-test against the EV null.
paper_tests.result_description: The fourfold pattern of risk (apparent probability weighting) appears in deterministic mirrors just as it does in lotteries, with the mirror pattern being about 97% as strong as the lottery pattern on average across fourfold tasks.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=2.2131, p=0.0000 | The paper tests whether mirror lambda significantly exceeds the loss-neutral benchmark of 1.0 using a Wilcoxon signed-rank test (approximated here as one-sample t-test) on the mirror loss aversion parameter against the null of lambda=1, with the reported mean mirror lambda of ~1.4 for A10.
paper_tests.result_description: Apparent loss aversion (lambda > 1) appears in deterministic mirrors even though mirrors cannot produce actual losses, with the mirror lambda being about 80% as strong as the lottery lambda.

paper_tests.description: OLS (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper reports a cross-subject Pearson correlation between each subject's mean absolute deviation from EV in the lottery treatment and their mean absolute deviation from EV in the mirror treatment, which is equivalent to an OLS regression of one on the other (standardized), testing whether the cross-subject correlation r=0.68 is significantly different from zero.
paper_tests.result_description: Across subjects, the severity of each anomaly in lotteries is strongly predicted by its severity in deterministic mirrors, suggesting a common behavioral mechanism unrelated to risk preferences.

description: Oprea (2024) investigates whether classical anomalies in risky decision-making — such as probability weighting and loss aversion — arise from risk itself or from the complexity of processing probabilistic outcomes. Participants value a series of monetary prospects using multiple price lists, indicating the minimum sure payment they would accept in exchange for each prospect. The key manipulation is whether each prospect is a genuine lottery — where one outcome is selected at random — or a "deterministic mirror" that pays the arithmetic average of all possible outcomes with certainty, eliminating any actual risk while preserving the same numerical complexity. The outcome is the deviation of each valuation from the prospect's expected value, which captures whether participants systematically over- or under-price prospects in ways that mirror classical biases. The original study finds that the fourfold pattern of risk attitudes and loss aversion appear nearly as strongly in the deterministic mirrors as in the lotteries — and that individual severity of these anomalies is highly correlated across both treatments — suggesting that what looks like risk preference is largely a response to complexity rather than uncertainty itself.

outcome_variables.name: Deviation from Expected Value (CE/SE)
outcome_variables.scale: MPL with $1 steps from $0 to $25 (gains) or -$25 to $0 (losses)

outcome_variables.name: Loss Aversion Parameter (Lambda)
outcome_variables.scale: MPL over 50/50 lotteries (X, -$10) or (X, -$15) with $X increasing in $2 steps

outcome_variables.name: Deviation From Expected Value Relative to Pattern-Consistent Deviation

title: Beyond Truth-Telling: Preference Estimation with Centralized School Choice and College Admissions
paper_brief: Challenges the truth-telling assumption in centralized school choice and proposes a weaker stability-based criterion; tests whether students submit strategic rank-order lists that omit infeasible top-ranked schools when priority scores fall below admission cutoffs.

paper_tests.description: t-test (Welch) (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0075, p=0.1561 | The paper compares the proportion of students including the most selective school in their rank-order list between two groups (priority above vs. below cutoff), which corresponds to a two-sample t-test on the binary outcome 'includes_top_school' across the two conditions; the original effect is 0.83 - 0.38 = 0.45.
paper_tests.result_description: Students with priority scores below the admission cutoff of the most selective school systematically omit that school from their rank-order lists, consistent with strategic 'skipping the impossible' rather than truthful reporting.

paper_tests.description: t-test (Welch) (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.0075, p=0.1561 | The paper compares prediction accuracy (includes_top_school) between students whose priority score is above vs. below the cutoff, mirroring the out-of-sample prediction comparison between stability and truth-telling estimators, with the reported effect being the difference in means (0.83 - 0.38 = 0.45).
paper_tests.result_description: The stability-based preference estimator outperforms the truth-telling estimator in predicting actual admission outcomes and student preferences in the Paris data.

description: Fack, Grenet, and He (2019) investigate whether students behave strategically when submitting ranked lists of preferred high schools under a centralized admissions system — specifically, whether they truthfully rank all schools they want or instead omit schools they believe are out of reach. Participants are middle school students in Paris applying to academic-track high schools, who submit a ranked list of schools as part of the official admissions process run by a deferred acceptance algorithm. The key manipulation compares students whose academic priority scores place them above the admission cutoff of the most selective school in their choice set against students whose scores fall below that cutoff — a distinction that determines whether gaining admission to that school is realistically feasible. The outcome is whether students include the most selective school in their submitted rank-order list, which captures the degree to which their reported preferences reflect genuine desires versus calculated omissions of seemingly unattainable options. The original study finds that students below the admission cutoff systematically omit the most selective school from their lists at substantially higher rates than students above the cutoff, rejecting the truth-telling assumption in favor of a stability-based model — one that accounts for strategic "skipping of the impossible" — and showing that this stability model also outperforms the truth-telling model in predicting actual admission outcomes.

design_summary.sample_type: middle school students applying to academic-track high schools in Paris

outcome_variables.name: Most Selective School Included in Ranking

title: Neighborhood-Based Information Costs
paper_brief: Derives neighborhood-based information cost functions that predict smooth psychometric-function response frequencies in binary choice—unlike mutual information which predicts step functions—and applies these to perceptual judgment, regime-change games, and LQG settings.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.5000, p=0.0000 | The key theoretical prediction is that p(larger) = 0.5 exactly at the threshold stimulus (65), so a one-sample t-test against 0.5 for the stim_65 condition directly tests the central identifying feature of the smooth sigmoid psychometric function at the threshold.
paper_tests.result_description: Under neighborhood-based (Fisher information) costs, response frequencies in binary threshold classification follow a smooth sigmoid psychometric function—strictly increasing from lower asymptote (~0.04) to upper asymptote (~0.96) with maximum slope at the threshold. This contrasts with the mutual information prediction of a discrete jump at the threshold (near-step function), which is inconsistent with the psychophysics and regime-change game experimental evidence.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.5000, p=0.0000 | The key theoretical prediction is that at the threshold stimulus (65), the psychometric function passes through 0.5 (50% probability of responding 'larger'), so a one-sample t-test against 0.5 on the stim_65 condition directly tests whether the simulated data replicates this central prediction of the smooth psychometric function.
paper_tests.result_description: In regime-change games, neighborhood-based costs predict a unique monotone equilibrium where each agent's investment probability is a smooth psychometric function of the fundamental, consistent with laboratory evidence from Heinemann, Nagel, and Ockenfels (2009) and Szkup and Trevino (2020). Mutual information yields multiplicity of equilibria despite imperfect observation (Yang 2015).

description: Hébert and Woodford (2021) investigate whether a new class of information cost functions — called neighborhood-based costs, which assume that nearby states of the world are harder to distinguish than distant ones — better explains how people make decisions under limited attention than the standard mutual information approach. Participants in the referenced experiments perform binary classification tasks, judging whether a stimulus is above or below a threshold, or decide whether to invest based on an underlying economic fundamental. The key manipulation is the value of the stimulus or fundamental presented — ranging from well below the threshold to well above it — allowing researchers to trace out how decision probabilities shift across the full range of inputs. The outcome is the proportion of times participants choose the "larger" or "invest" option, which captures how smoothly and systematically behavior responds to changes in the underlying state — a pattern that reveals whether attention is allocated in a way consistent with neighborhood-based or mutual information costs. The original study finds that neighborhood-based costs predict a smooth, S-shaped response curve that rises gradually from near zero to near one as the stimulus moves away from the threshold, matching the psychophysical and regime-change game evidence, while the mutual information model incorrectly predicts a sharp, near-stepwise jump at the threshold and — in the investment game — generates multiple possible equilibria rather than the single, well-behaved equilibrium observed in the lab.

design_summary.sample_type: theoretical model; references psychophysics and regime-change game laboratory subjects from prior work

title: Intrinsic Information Preferences and Skewness
paper_brief: Examines intrinsic preferences for positively versus negatively skewed information structures, finding a widespread preference for positive skew and showing that positively skewed signals can reduce information avoidance.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.5000, p=0.0000 | The paper reports a two-sided binomial test against H0: 50% for the proportion choosing positive skew in T2, which is equivalent to a one-sample t-test of the binary outcome (infochoice_numeric=1) against null value 0.5.
paper_tests.result_description: Strong preference for positively skewed over negatively skewed information in T2: 79% choose (0.5,1) over (1,0.5) of equal variance, p<0.001, information premium 20.5 cents.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.5000, p=0.0000 | The paper reports a two-sided binomial test against H0: 50% for the proportion choosing positive skew in T3, which is best approximated as a one-sample t-test of the binary outcome variable against null value 0.5 for the T3 condition subset.
paper_tests.result_description: Preference for positively skewed over negatively skewed information in T3: 67% choose (0.3,0.9) over (0.9,0.3), p=0.002, information premium 7.5 cents.

paper_tests.description: one-sample t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=-0.5000, p=0.0000 | The paper reports a two-sided binomial test against H0: 50% for the proportion choosing positive skew in T4, which is best approximated by a one-sample t-test of the binary outcome variable against null value 0.5 for the T4 condition subset.
paper_tests.result_description: Preference for positively skewed over negatively skewed information in T4: 74% choose (0.6,0.9) over (0.9,0.6), p<0.001, information premium 12.3 cents.

paper_tests.description: t-test (Welch) (LLM-resolved): effect=0.0000, p=nan | The paper reports two-sided chi-squared tests comparing proportions of information-takers between conditions (T5 vs T1: 87% vs 70%, p=0.011; T7 vs T1: 72% vs 70%, p=0.771), which for a binary outcome is equivalent to a two-sample t-test on the binary infochoice_numeric variable between the two condition groups.
paper_tests.result_description: Information avoidance is lower for positively skewed signals than for full information: 13% avoid in T5 vs 30% in T1 (p=0.011), but no reduction for negatively skewed signals (T7: 28% avoid, T1 vs T7: p=0.771).

description: Masatlioglu, Orhun, and Raymond (2023) investigate whether people have intrinsic preferences for the *shape* of information — specifically, whether they prefer signals that deliver rare but highly diagnostic good news over signals that deliver rare but highly diagnostic bad news, independent of any practical value the information provides. Participants are offered a choice between two information structures about a binary outcome — such as winning or losing a prize — where each structure determines how likely a good or bad signal is to arrive and how strongly that signal predicts the true outcome. The key manipulation is the *skewness* of the signal: a positively skewed structure makes good news rare but highly informative, while a negatively skewed structure makes bad news rare but highly informative, with the mean and variance of the structures held constant so that any preference reflects something beyond standard expected-utility reasoning. The outcome is the participant's chosen information structure, which captures their intrinsic taste for how uncertainty is resolved — revealing whether people care not just about *how much* they learn, but *how* good or bad news is distributed across signals. The original study finds a robust preference for positively skewed information: across multiple conditions, roughly two-thirds to nearly four-fifths of participants choose the positively skewed option, and participants are willing to pay a meaningful premium for it, while information avoidance is also significantly lower when the available signal is positively rather than negatively skewed.

title: Adverse and Advantageous Selection in the Laboratory
paper_brief: Investigates whether laboratory subjects account for adverse and advantageous selection in voting games, finding that most players fail to apply iterated dominance reasoning even when strategic uncertainty is removed.

paper_tests.description: Mann-Whitney U (LLM-resolved): effect=0.9302, p=0.0000 | The paper reports a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-rank test comparing uninformed players' risky vote rates between positively and negatively correlated conditions with the $12 safe option; since the simulated data has one observation per subject per condition (not repeated measures paired within subject), this is implemented as a Mann-Whitney U test between ai_12P and ai_12N on the vote_risky outcome, with the original effect being the difference in means (0.860 - 0.477 = 0.383).
paper_tests.result_description: Uninformed players vote for the Risky option significantly more often when payoffs are positively correlated than negatively correlated with the $12 safe option, consistent with accounting for adverse and advantageous selection.

paper_tests.description: Mann-Whitney U (LLM-resolved): effect=0.8953, p=0.0000 | The paper reports a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-rank test comparing risky vote rates between ai_16N and ai_16P conditions; since the simulated data has one observation per subject per condition (not repeated measures paired within subjects), the equivalent non-parametric two-sample test is the Mann-Whitney U test, with the effect being the difference in means (0.326 - 0.012 = 0.314).
paper_tests.result_description: Uninformed players vote for the Risky option significantly more often when payoffs are positively correlated than negatively correlated with the $16 safe option.

paper_tests.description: paired t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper uses a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-rank test comparing negative vs. positive correlation conditions within subjects; since each subject appears in both conditions, this is a paired comparison, and the closest parametric equivalent for replication purposes is a paired t-test comparing vote_risky between negative and positive correlation AI conditions (e.g., ai_12N vs ai_12P) across the same subjects.
paper_tests.result_description: Subjects respond more to adverse selection than advantageous selection: in the AI game, more than half consistently choose Safe in negatively correlated rounds but fewer than one-third consistently choose Risky in positively correlated rounds.

paper_tests.description: Mann-Whitney U (LLM-resolved): effect=0.0000, p=1.0000 | The paper uses a Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-rank test to compare AI vs SI conditions; since the simulated data lacks a within-subject pairing structure (each agent appears once per condition independently), the equivalent non-parametric two-sample test is the Mann-Whitney U test, comparing vote_risky between ai_12N and si_12N (difference = 0.477 - 0.779 = -0.302).
paper_tests.result_description: Behavior in the AI game differs substantially from the SI game, confirming subjects react to the presence of asymmetric information. AI 12N is much lower than SI 12N (adverse selection effect), and AI 16P is much higher than SI 16P (advantageous selection effect).

description: Ali, Mihm, Siga, and Tergiman (2021) investigate whether ordinary people account for adverse and advantageous selection — the strategic distortions that arise when one party in a decision has private information the other lacks. Participants play a two-player voting game in which one player (Alice) is informed about the outcome of a lottery while the other (Bob) is not; both must vote simultaneously for either a Safe option with a fixed payout or a Risky option that pays out based on the lottery, and the Risky option is only implemented if both players vote for it. The key manipulation is the correlation between Alice's private information and the Safe option's value — in some conditions the Safe option is relatively low, meaning Alice tends to vote Risky precisely when the lottery looks good (advantageous selection for Bob), while in other conditions the Safe option is relatively high, meaning Alice votes Risky mainly when the lottery looks bad (adverse selection against Bob). The outcome is Bob's vote for the Risky option, which captures whether uninformed players reason about what an informed player's willingness to vote reveals about the hidden lottery outcome. The original study finds that uninformed players vote for the Risky option far more often under advantageous selection than adverse selection, and that behavior shifts substantially compared to a benchmark where both players are equally uninformed — confirming that participants genuinely respond to the strategic implications of asymmetric information, though they react more strongly to adverse selection than to advantageous selection.
